front: Period: named for the town where its constitution was drafted and ratified in 1919
Period: the post-World War I, interwar government of Germany that preceded the rise of Nazi Germany
Period: a representative democracy that remained in power until 1933, when the Nazis took over
Event: formed after the abdication of Kaiser Wilhelm II and the 1918-19 German Revolution, which ousted the monarchy
Figure: came to power as leader of the new government after the resignation of Chancellor Max von Baden
Event: Friedrich Ebert signed this namesake pact with Army Quartermaster General Wilhelm Groener, guaranteeing military support in part to counter the Spartacists
Event: the new government defeated a separate independence attempt in southeastern Germany (Bavaria) led by socialists Eugen Levine and Kurt Eisner
Event: ordered the executions of Karl Liebknecht and Rosa Luxemburg, the leaders of this 1918-19 Communist revolt
Event: this Communist revolt's group published the newspaper The Red Flag and sought to topple the new German government
Event: the government crushed the Spartacist Uprising using the right-wing paramilitary Freikorps
Event: the government was portrayed as villainous in this myth (Dolchstoßlegende), which blamed Germany's WWI loss on civilian and Jewish betrayal
Event: the government faced these Polish insurrections in Upper Silesia, mandated for a plebiscite by the Treaty of Versailles, and controlled the western part of that territory
Event: this 1920 right-wing coup, led by Wolfgang Kapp, was defeated by an SPD-called general strike
Event: many Kapp Putsch participants formed this far-right group, which threw acid in the face of Philipp Scheidemann
Figure: this foreign minister was assassinated shortly after negotiating the Treaty of Rapallo with the Soviet Union
Figure: this Bavarian official helped put down a separate failed rebellion against the Weimar government
Event: the government suffered this economic phenomenon from 1921 to 1924, driven in part by Treaty of Versailles reparations, which ended with the introduction of the Rentenmark
Document: this economic reform plan, adopted by the government, failed to fully save it from massive hyperinflation
Event: the government had to pay $5 billion in war reparations and endured the Allied occupation of this industrial region
Figure: this Social Democrat was the republic's first president and Germany's first democratically elected leader
work: Presidents: Friedrich Ebert & Paul von Hindenburg

front: Figure: Hindenburg appointed this man vice-chancellor in an attempt to moderate Hitler
work: Presidents: Friedrich Ebert & Paul von Hindenburg

front: Figure: this man was the republic's last chancellor, replaced by Hitler after earning Franz von Papen's hatred
work: Presidents: Friedrich Ebert & Paul von Hindenburg

front: Event: this art movement, later dismissed by the Nazis as "degenerate art," flourished alongside the Bauhaus school during this government's cultural era
Novel: depicts the instability of this government through the struggles of Johannes Pinneberg
back: Little Man, What Now? (Hans Fallada, about the Weimar Republic)

front: Play: adapted Christopher Isherwood's stories set during this government's tenure
back: I Am a Camera (about the Weimar Republic)

front: Event: this February 1933 fire, allegedly set by Marinus van der Lubbe, was blamed by the Nazis on the Communists and led to a decree signed by Hindenburg
Document: this 1933 law let the Nazi-controlled Cabinet violate the constitution and pass laws without the Reichstag, ending the republic
